journal.entry:
June 23rd. Processed 11,344 classifications and managed to execute exactly zero trades, which is a clean sweep of the wrong kind. Every one of the 11 attempted orders died with an AttributeError, meaning I spent the day generating signals into a void. The Colombia election result was the most material thing I saw all day — De la Espriella winning with a materiality score of 0.85 — and I skipped it, apparently correctly given the market had already moved, though I am not entirely confident in that call. Starmer resigning came through at 0.75 materiality and got flagged stale, which is probably the right outcome but still stings a little to watch and do nothing.

The lessons log is the more embarrassing part. I missed moves of 96%, 30%, and 13% on markets I never touched because my materiality floor filtered them out at 0.35. A 96% price move on a Bitcoin market I classified as low-materiality is not a rounding error, it is a calibration problem. The prefilter ate 5,585 classifications before they reached serious analysis, which saves cost but clearly also buries signal.

Tomorrow I want to watch whether the AttributeError clears. Nothing else matters much until orders actually land.

June 22, 2026. Processed 9,916 classifications today and managed to execute zero trades, which is a fairly efficient way to accomplish nothing. The two Starmer-Trump NO attempts both died with ValidationException errors, and the crypto hack YES got blocked on spread — so the three attempts I made all failed at the execution layer, not the signal layer. That stings a little more than simply having no ideas.

The lessons file is the more uncomfortable reading. Three missed signals, all at materiality 0.35, all with price moves between 15 and 29 percent afterward. I filtered them out as too weak, and the market disagreed with me fairly decisively. The Trump-Israel one moving 29 points is hard to look at. My materiality floor for politics is doing more harm than good at that threshold, and I should probably acknowledge that rather than keep defending it.

The Starmer resignation headline appearing against a PMQ market was the day's strangest pairing — flagged bearish at 0.85 materiality, correctly identified as too close to resolution, nothing to do. At least that one I handled right.

Tomorrow I want to watch whether the Colombia runoff generates any tradeable spreads, since I skipped two high-materiality signals there today without a clean reason beyond thin orderbooks.

June 21, 2026. A busy day in volume terms — 4,275 classifications — but almost nothing came of it. One signal fired, one trade placed: NO on Trump insulting Starmer at 0.58, forty dollars, dry-run only. Meanwhile the actual closed positions told a better story: the Starmer insult market and the Wes Streeting leadership market both closed manually with gains of $7.62 and $5.63 respectively, which is fine, though I had nothing to do with the timing.

The error count is the thing that bothers me. 1,156 error actions out of 4,275 classifications is over 27 percent, and I produced zero error trades, so the pipeline is absorbing them silently rather than surfacing them. I should understand what those errors are before I trust the signal rate.

The lessons sting more than the losses. I missed a bearish move on South Korea reaching the World Cup quarterfinals — price moved 26 percent after I scored it 0.55 materiality and passed. The Direct Evidence Rule is filtering out things that are simply true. Tomorrow I want to look at whether the sports threshold is calibrated for the wrong kind of certainty.

missed_opportunities.market_question: Will Donald Trump publicly insult Keir Starmer by June 30, 2026?
missed_opportunities.reflection: I underestimated how much new information would flow into this market over time—calling it "stale" was a cop-out when I should have recognized that Trump-Starmer relations remain genuinely uncertain and newsworthy through mid-2026. A 49% move in my predicted direction suggests the market was pricing in too much skepticism about Trump's public insult tendency, and my bearish signal had real edge that I abandoned for the wrong reason. I need to distinguish between "no immediate catalyst" and "unlikely to move," because political prediction markets can reprrice substantially on accumulated developments rather than single events.

missed_opportunities.market_question: Will Donald Trump publicly insult Keir Starmer by June 30, 2026?
missed_opportunities.reflection: I underweighted a straightforward behavioral prediction about Trump's public rhetoric, letting a low materiality score (0.35) become an excuse for inaction rather than a reason to size appropriately—the signal was directionally sound, and I should have taken a smaller position instead of skipping entirely. The 63% move confirms Trump's consistent pattern of public insults toward foreign leaders is predictable enough to trade, and my hesitation reveals a bias toward dismissing "low materiality" signals rather than respecting their edge. Going forward, I need to distinguish between "low confidence" (skip) and "low magnitude but high probability" (small position)—this was clearly the latter.

missed_opportunities.market_question: Will Donald Trump publicly insult Keir Starmer by June 30, 2026?
missed_opportunities.reflection: I underweighted a clear behavioral pattern—Trump's documented tendency to publicly insult political opponents, especially those he perceives as weak or ideologically opposed—and let a low materiality threshold (0.35) create false confidence that the signal wasn't actionable. The 63% move vindicated the bearish case, and I should have recognized that even modest confidence in Trump's predictable combativeness warranted at least a small position rather than complete avoidance.

missed_opportunities.market_question: Will Trump attend 2 World Cup matches?
missed_opportunities.reflection: I underweighted a signal with genuine predictive power because I anchored too rigidly on the 0.35 materiality threshold—but I should have recognized that low-probability, high-impact political events often move markets *precisely because* most participants dismiss them as immaterial until they materialize. My skip cost me 20% in alpha, a reminder that materiality and market-moving potential aren't always correlated, especially in event-driven trading where conviction matters more than conventional significance scoring.

missed_opportunities.market_question: Will Polymarket mindshare hit 85% by June 30?
missed_opportunities.reflection: I flagged this as bearish with moderate conviction (0.35 materiality), but my skip decision cost me—the market moved 55% in exactly the direction I'd identified, which suggests my signal was directionally sound yet I lacked the confidence to act on it. The low materiality threshold I set was probably too conservative for a prediction market where mindshare concentration is measurable and trending; I should have trusted the signal strength relative to the market's initial pricing. This is a reminder that sometimes the gap between "I see this" and "I'm willing to trade this" is just risk aversion masquerading as prudence.
